New South Wales.subtitle: Australia's most populous state ┬╖ 5.1M enrolled students

issues.name: Cyberbullying & Online Harm
issues.desc: NSW accounts for the largest absolute number of cyberbullying victims. eSafety data shows over half of 10ΓÇô17 year olds have experienced cyberbullying at some point.

issues.name: Anxiety Disorders
issues.desc: Anxiety disorders are the most common mental health condition among NSW school-aged children, with urban social pressure compounding exposure.

issues.name: School Refusal
issues.desc: Parliamentary Library research highlights a significant post-pandemic spike in school refusal driven by anxiety and social disconnection, with NSW schools reporting elevated chronic absence.

issues.name: Sleep Deprivation
issues.desc: Half of older teens statewide do not meet sleep guidelines on school nights, impairing concentration and emotional regulation.

issues.name: School Belonging Deficits
issues.desc: NSW CESE finds strong links between belonging and lower bullying. Scores declined post-COVID across many NSW schools.

Victoria.subtitle: Highest youth mental health service demand nationally

issues.name: Depression & Psychological Distress
issues.desc: Mission Australia Youth Survey 2024 identifies one in five Victorian teens with high psychological distress. Victoria has the highest demand on youth mental health services nationally.

issues.name: School Belonging
issues.desc: Belonging and connectedness scores have declined significantly in Victorian secondary schools since COVID, linked to hybrid learning disruption and social withdrawal.

issues.name: Loneliness
issues.desc: Over one in five young Victorians report feeling lonely most or all of the time ΓÇö a key predictor of depression and disengagement from school.

issues.name: Online Hate & Harmful Content
issues.desc: eSafety research documents high rates of exposure to hate and harmful content among Victorian teens active on social platforms.

Queensland.subtitle: State with most documented school bullying incidents nationally

issues.name: Bullying in Schools
issues.desc: The Queensland Auditor-General (Report 6, 2024ΓÇô25) recorded over 46,000 bullying and harassment incidents in Queensland state schools in 2023 ΓÇö the most documented in any Australian jurisdiction.

issues.name: Attendance Decline
issues.desc: Attendance drops sharply in Queensland's outer regional and remote zones per RoGS 2026, with significant equity implications for students away from the south-east.

issues.name: Cyberbullying
issues.desc: Cyberbullying crosses between school and digital spaces. Queensland schools are adapting policies, but online harm remains a critical ongoing challenge.

issues.name: Racism & Discrimination
issues.desc: Discrimination is a top concern among Queensland youth, particularly in outer suburban and regional schools per Mission Australia 2024.

issues.name: Stress & Academic Pressure
issues.desc: ATAR pressure intensifies in Years 11ΓÇô12. Queensland students show elevated stress-related learning barriers in PISA 2022 Australia data.

South Australia.subtitle: Significant health equity and mental health access gaps

issues.name: Psychological Distress & Loneliness
issues.desc: South Australian teens mirror the national Mission Australia finding ΓÇö one in five report high distress. Rural SA has notably fewer support services available.

issues.name: Anxiety & Depression
issues.desc: AIHW Youth Self-Harm Atlas maps elevated depression/anxiety risk in several SA PHN regions, particularly Eyre-Far North and Country SA.

issues.name: Sleep Deprivation
issues.desc: Between one quarter (younger teens) and half (older teens) of SA students fail to meet sleep guidelines, with direct impacts on learning capacity.

issues.name: School Disengagement
issues.desc: SA secondary school attendance and engagement rates show consistent decline particularly in Years 9ΓÇô10, mirroring national patterns.

Western Australia.subtitle: Vast geography creates Australia's most extreme wellbeing inequities

issues.name: Remote Attendance Crisis
issues.desc: RoGS 2026 data shows attendance in very remote WA schools can fall below 60%, compared to 93% in major cities ΓÇö the steepest gradient nationally.

issues.name: Indigenous Self-Harm & Suicidality
issues.desc: AIHW Youth Self-Harm Atlas shows WA regional areas consistently in the highest national percentile bands for youth self-harm, with Aboriginal communities most affected.

issues.name: Racism & Cultural Exclusion
issues.desc: Discrimination is a documented barrier to school engagement for Aboriginal and culturally diverse students in WA, per Mission Australia 2024.

issues.name: Motivation & Disengagement
issues.desc: Low motivation is structurally linked to geographic isolation, limited extracurriculars, and teacher shortages in regional and remote WA.

Tasmania.subtitle: High disadvantage linked to multiple overlapping wellbeing vulnerabilities

issues.name: Socioeconomic Disadvantage & Distress
issues.desc: Tasmania consistently records Australia's lowest socioeconomic indicators (SEIFA), directly correlating with elevated mental health challenges, stress, and family instability.

issues.name: Sleep Deprivation
issues.desc: Tasmanian adolescents show elevated rates of poor sleep, with cold winters, screen use, and disadvantage all contributing factors.

issues.name: School Belonging
issues.desc: Tasmanian students score below the national average on belonging measures, with small-school isolation as a significant contributing factor.

issues.name: Stress & Academic Pressure
issues.desc: The Tasmanian Certificate of Education creates significant stress in Years 11ΓÇô12, with limited academic support outside Hobart and Launceston.

Northern Territory.subtitle: Deepest attendance and self-harm crisis in Australia

issues.name: Attendance Crisis
issues.desc: The NT has the lowest school attendance rates in Australia. RoGS 2026 documents remote NT schools averaging well below 60% attendance ΓÇö denying children their fundamental right to education.

issues.name: Indigenous Self-Harm & Suicidality
issues.desc: AIHW Youth Self-Harm Atlas data shows NT regional areas consistently in the highest national percentile bands for youth self-harm and suicidality, with Aboriginal communities most affected.

issues.name: Psychological Distress
issues.desc: Poverty, housing instability, family disruption, and limited mental health services create compounding distress for NT children, particularly in remote communities.

issues.name: Racism & Cultural Disconnect
issues.desc: The disconnect between Western schooling frameworks and Aboriginal cultural contexts remains a significant barrier to engagement, belonging, and mental safety for NT students.

Australian Capital Territory.subtitle: High-achieving culture masks significant hidden anxiety burden

issues.name: Anxiety & Academic Stress
issues.desc: ACT students, attending Australia's highest-performing school system by PISA measures, report some of the highest stress and anxiety around academic expectations, particularly Years 9ΓÇô12.

issues.name: Workload & Burnout
issues.desc: ACT students face elevated exposure to academic workload stressors, with high-income, high-expectation environments intensifying the pressure placed on young people.

issues.name: Cyberbullying
issues.desc: Despite smaller population, ACT records cyberbullying prevalence consistent with national urban averages ΓÇö amplified by tight social networks where incidents have concentrated impacts.

issues.name: Sleep Deprivation
issues.desc: Screen time and academic pressure combine to create sleep debt in ACT teens, consistent with national AIHW data showing half of 16ΓÇô17 year olds miss sleep guidelines.
